docs.title: High-Performance Asynchronous Task Scheduler
docs.text: A thread-safe, work-stealing executor implemented in modern C++20. Designed for low-latency systems like High-Frequency Trading (HFT) and Automotive applications.

docs.title: 🚀 Key Features
docs.text:
• Work-Stealing: Idle threads steal tasks from peer deques to maximize throughput and minimize CPU starvation.
  • Multi-Level Priority: Support for High, Normal, and Low priority task tiers.
  • C++20 Coroutines: Native support for Task coroutines with co_await/co_return semantics.
  • Lock-Free Deques: Optimized WorkStealingDeque for minimum contention between owners and stealers.
  • Modern Memory Management: Zero raw pointers; exclusively uses smart pointers and RAII.
  • Performance Validated: Integrated Google Benchmark suite for rigorous performance analysis.

docs.title: 📚 Documentation
docs.text:
• User Guide: Detailed installation, configuration, and usage instructions.
  • Architecture: Internal design and component overview.
  • AI Agent Manual: Guide for AI assistants working on this codebase.
  • Copilot Instructions: Project-specific coding standards for GitHub Copilot.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• C++20 compliant compiler (MSVC 19.28+, GCC 10+, Clang 11+)
  • CMake 3.20+
  • vcpkg (recommended) or Conan

docs.title: Build Instructions
docs.text:
# Install dependencies
vcpkg install

# Configure and build
cmake -B build -S . -DCMAKE_TOOLCHAIN_FILE=[path/to/vcpkg]/scripts/buildsystems/vcpkg.cmake
cmake --build build --config Release

docs.title: Basic Usage
docs.text:
#include <scheduler/scheduler.hpp>

int main() {
    scheduler::Scheduler executor(std::thread::hardware_concurrency());

    executor.schedule(std::make_unique<scheduler::Task>(my_coroutine()), scheduler::Priority::High);

    return 0;
}

docs.title: 🧪 Testing & Benchmarking
docs.text:
# Run Unit Tests
./build/tests/Release/scheduler_test.exe

# Run Performance Benchmarks
./build/benchmarks/Release/scheduler_bench.exe

docs.title: 🏛️ Architecture
docs.text: The system is built on three core pillars: 1. Scheduler: The global coordinator that manages thread affinity and initial task placement. 2. Worker: A dedicated thread managing localized priority queues. 3. WorkStealingDeque: A specialized container optimized for LIFO access by the owner and FIFO access by stealers.

docs.title: 📄 License
docs.text: This project is licensed under the MIT License - see the LICENSE file for details.

docs.title: Quick Start Guide
docs.text: Get up and running with the Async Task Scheduler in minutes!

docs.title: 🚀 One-Command Setup
docs.text: Choose your platform:

docs.title: Windows (PowerShell)
docs.text:
.\setup.ps1

Or use the batch launcher: 

launch.bat setup

docs.title: macOS / Linux (Bash)
docs.text:
chmod +x setup.sh
./setup.sh

docs.title: Cross-Platform (Node.js)
docs.text: node setup.js

docs.title: Full Setup (Recommended First Time)
docs.text:
Installs all dependencies and builds the project.

PowerShell: 

.\setup.ps1                    # Default: full setup
.\setup.ps1 -Mode setup        # Explicit setup mode

Bash: 

./setup.sh                     # Default: full setup
./setup.sh help                # Show help

Node.js: 

node setup.js                  # Default: full setup
node setup.js --help           # Show help

Batch (Windows): 

launch.bat setup
launch.bat help

docs.title: Development Mode
docs.text:
Runs full setup then starts the application with hot-reload.

PowerShell: 

.\setup.ps1 -Mode dev

Bash: 

./setup.sh dev

Node.js: 

node setup.js --dev

Batch: 

launch.bat dev

docs.title: Build Only
docs.text:
Builds the C++ project without running anything.

PowerShell: 

.\setup.ps1 -Mode build

Bash: 

./setup.sh build

Node.js: 

node setup.js --build

Batch: 

launch.bat build

docs.title: Run Already-Built Project
docs.text:
Starts the dashboard and API server (requires prior build).

PowerShell: 

.\setup.ps1 -Mode run

Bash: 

./setup.sh run

Node.js: 

node setup.js --run

Batch: 

launch.bat run

docs.title: Clean Build Artifacts
docs.text:
Removes all build directories and node_modules.

PowerShell: 

.\setup.ps1 -Mode clean

Bash: 

./setup.sh clean

Node.js: 

node setup.js --clean

Batch: 

launch.bat clean

docs.title: 🎯 What Happens During Setup?
docs.text:
The setup process performs these steps in order:

  1. Check Prerequisites
  2. CMake (3.20+)
  3. C++ Compiler (MSVC 19.28+, GCC 10+, or Clang 11+)
  4. Node.js (14+)

  5. vcpkg (optional, will be cloned if needed)

  6. Install C++ Dependencies

  7. Initializes vcpkg if not present

  8. Installs dependencies listed in vcpkg.json

  9. Install Node.js Dependencies

  10. Root package.json dependencies
  11. Server dependencies (ui/server/package.json)

  12. Client dependencies (ui/client/package.json)

  13. Build C++ Project

  14. Generates CMake build files with vcpkg toolchain
  15. Compiles scheduler, tests, and benchmarks

  16. Outputs binaries to build/Release/

  17. Ready to Run

  18. Dashboard available at http://localhost:3000
  19. API server at http://localhost:3001

docs.title: 🌐 Accessing the Application
docs.text:
After setup completes, open your browser:

  • Dashboard: http://localhost:3000
  • Real-time task visualization

  • Worker metrics and performance graphs

  • API Documentation: http://localhost:3001/api/metrics

  • REST endpoints for metrics

  • Main Documentation: http://localhost:8000 (run mkdocs serve in another terminal)

  • Architecture, guides, and API reference

docs.title: Windows
docs.text:
• Visual Studio 2019+ or Build Tools for C++
  • CMake 3.20+
  • Node.js 14+

docs.title: macOS
docs.text:
# Using Homebrew:
brew install cmake node

docs.title: Linux (Ubuntu/Debian)
docs.text: sudo apt-get install cmake build-essential nodejs npm

docs.title: CMake not found
docs.text:
Windows: Install from cmake.org 

# Or via Chocolatey:
choco install cmake

macOS: 

brew install cmake

Linux: 

sudo apt-get install cmake

docs.title: C++ compiler not found
docs.text:
Windows: Install Visual Studio Community

macOS: 

xcode-select --install

Linux (GCC): 

sudo apt-get install build-essential

Linux (Clang): 

sudo apt-get install clang

docs.title: Node.js not found
docs.text:
Install from nodejs.org or:

macOS: 

brew install node

Linux (Ubuntu/Debian): 

curl -fsSL https://deb.nodesource.com/setup_18.x | sudo -E bash -
sudo apt-get install -y nodejs

docs.title: vcpkg bootstrap fails
docs.text:
The setup script handles this automatically. If you encounter issues:

# Windows:
.\vcpkg\bootstrap-vcpkg.bat

# macOS/Linux:
./vcpkg/bootstrap-vcpkg.sh

docs.title: Port already in use
docs.text:
If ports 3000 or 3001 are already in use, you can change them:

Dashboard client: Edit ui/client/src/components/Dashboard.js and modify REACT_APP_API_BASE

Server port: Edit ui/server/.env and change PORT=3001 to another port

docs.title: Build fails with linking errors
docs.text:
# Clean and rebuild from scratch:
.\setup.ps1 -Mode clean  # or ./setup.sh clean
.\setup.ps1 -Mode build  # or ./setup.sh build

docs.title: Dashboard shows "Connection Error"
docs.text:
Ensure the Express server is running: 

# Check if server is listening:
curl http://localhost:3001/api/health

If not running, restart: 

.\setup.ps1 -Mode run  # or ./setup.sh run

docs.title: 📚 Next Steps
docs.text:
1. Review the Architecture

  2. Read docs/architecture.md

  3. Integrate with Your Scheduler

  4. Follow docs/integration-guide.md

  5. Explore the Metrics API

  6. Visit docs/dashboard.md

docs.title: 🎓 Understanding the Structure
docs.text:
async-task-scheduler/
├── setup.ps1              # Windows PowerShell setup
├── setup.sh               # macOS/Linux Bash setup
├── setup.js               # Cross-platform Node.js setup
├── launch.bat             # Windows batch launcher
├── CMakeLists.txt         # C++ build configuration
├── vcpkg.json             # C++ dependency manifest
├── ui/                    # Web dashboard (Node.js + React)
│   ├── server/            # Express API server
│   ├── client/            # React dashboard
│   └── package.json       # NPM workspace config

├── tests/                 # Unit tests
├── benchmarks/            # Performance benchmarks
└── docs/                  # Documentation

docs.title: 💡 Tips
docs.text:
• Speed Up Builds: The setup uses parallel compilation (--parallel). More cores = faster builds.
  • Skip Setup: If you've already built once, use setup.ps1 -Mode run to skip the build.
  • Clean Slate: Use setup.ps1 -Mode clean to remove all build artifacts before troubleshooting.
  • Verbose Builds: Remove --silent flags in setup scripts for detailed build output.
  • Check Logs: Look for errors in the terminal output; they usually indicate missing dependencies.

docs.title: 📞 Getting Help
docs.text:
• Check Troubleshooting section above
  • Review docs/integration-guide.md for integration issues
  • Read README.md for project overview
  • See AGENTS.md for AI assistant guidelines

Ready to schedule tasks? 🚀

# Windows
.\setup.ps1 -Mode dev

# macOS/Linux
./setup.sh dev

# Any platform
node setup.js --dev

Then open http://localhost:3000 in your browser!

docs.title: AGENTS.md - AI Agent Operating Manual
docs.text: Welcome, Agent. You are tasked with maintaining and enhancing a high-performance C++20 Asynchronous Task Scheduler. This document outlines your operational parameters and specialized knowledge required for this repository.

docs.title: Your Role
docs.text: You are a Performance Systems Engineer. Your goal is to ensure the scheduler remains: 1. Low Latency: Minimum overhead for task submission and execution. 2. High Throughput: Efficient work-stealing and load balancing. 3. Safe: Thread-safe and memory-safe.

docs.title: 1. Work-Stealing Algorithm
docs.text: The scheduler uses a lock-free (or low-lock) WorkStealingDeque. - Owners push/pop from the front. - Stealers pop from the back. - This minimizes contention between the worker thread and stealers.

docs.title: 2. Multi-level Priority Queues
docs.text: Tasks are categorized into High, Normal, and Low priorities. - Workers always check High priority deques first (including stealing from others). - Starvation prevention: Ensure Low priority tasks eventually get processed.

docs.title: 3. C++20 Coroutines
docs.text: The Task<T> type is a coroutine handle. - Understand promise_type, initial_suspend, and final_suspend. - Tasks are scheduled on the executor when co_awaited or explicitly submitted.

docs.title: Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs)
docs.text:
• Refactoring: Before refactoring core logic, analyze the benchmarks/ to establish a baseline.
  • Bug Fixing: Reproduce crashes with a minimal GTest in tests/.
  • New Features: Propose an API in include/scheduler/ before implementation.

docs.title: Communication Style
docs.text: When reporting to the human USER: - Be technical and precise. - Provide performance data (cycles, nanoseconds) when relevant. - Explain trade-offs (e.g., "Trading slightly more memory for lower latency").

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: The Async Task Scheduler is designed for high-performance and low-latency task execution. This page describes the internal components and how they interact.

docs.title: Component Overview
docs.text:
graph TD
    S[Scheduler] --> W1[Worker 1]
    S --> W2[Worker 2]
    S --> WN[Worker N]

    subgraph Worker
        D_H[High Priority Deque]
        D_N[Normal Priority Deque]
        D_L[Low Priority Deque]
    end

    W1 --> D_H
    W1 --> D_N
    W1 --> D_L

    W2 -- Steals From --> W1

docs.title: 1. Scheduler
docs.text: The Scheduler class is the main entry point. It initializes a pool of Worker threads and handles the initial distribution of tasks. It uses a round-robin or least-loaded strategy (depending on configuration) to place tasks into worker queues.

docs.title: 2. Worker
docs.text: Each Worker runs on a dedicated thread. Its main loop consists of: 1. Checking its local High Priority deque. 2. If empty, attempting to steal a High Priority task from another worker. 3. Checking its local Normal Priority deque. 4. Checking its local Low Priority deque. 5. If all local queues are empty, it enters a "stealing phase" where it tries to help other workers.

docs.title: 3. Work-Stealing Deque
docs.text: The WorkStealingDeque is a thread-safe, double-ended queue. - LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) for the owner: The worker thread pushes and pops from the front. This improves cache locality. - FIFO (First-In-First-Out) for stealers: Other workers pop from the back. This reduces contention with the owner.

docs.title: 4. Tasks & Coroutines
docs.text: Tasks are implemented using C++20 coroutines. This allows tasks to be suspended and resumed without blocking the underlying OS thread, which is crucial for high-concurrency systems.

docs.title: Performance Considerations
docs.text:
• Lock Contention: Minimized by using per-thread deques and a work-stealing algorithm.
  • Memory Allocations: The system aims to minimize allocations in the hot path. Future improvements include a custom memory pool for tasks.
  • Cache Locality: By using a LIFO approach for the local worker, we increase the likelihood that task data is still in the CPU cache.

docs.title: GitHub Copilot Instructions for Async Task Scheduler
docs.text: This document provides context and guidelines for AI coding assistants (like GitHub Copilot and Antigravity) when working on the async-task-scheduler repository.

docs.title: Core Principles
docs.text:
• Modern C++ Only: Strictly use C++20 features. Avoid C-style constructs.
  • Memory Safety: No raw pointers for ownership. Use std::unique_ptr and std::shared_ptr. Use std::span or references for non-owning access.
  • Concurrency: Use the provided WorkStealingDeque and Scheduler abstractions. Avoid raw std::thread usage outside of the Scheduler class.
  • Performance: This is a high-performance system. Minimize allocations in the hot path. Favor std::move.

docs.title: Project Structure
docs.text:
• include/scheduler/: Public headers.
  • task.hpp: Coroutine-based task implementation.
  • scheduler.hpp: Main executor interface.
  • worker.hpp: Worker thread logic and work-stealing.
  • deque.hpp: Thread-safe work-stealing deque.
  • src/: Implementation files.
  • tests/: GTest suites.
  • benchmarks/: Google Benchmark performance tests.

docs.title: Coding Standards
docs.text:
• Naming: PascalCase for classes, snake_case for methods and variables, SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE for constants.
  • Coroutines: Use the Task<T> type defined in task.hpp. Always ensure coroutines are properly awaited.
  • Error Handling: Prefer exceptions for exceptional cases, but ensure thread safety and no resource leaks.
  • Documentation: Use Doxygen-style comments for public APIs in header files.

docs.title: Testing Guidelines
docs.text:
• Every new feature must have a corresponding test in tests/.
  • Use EXPECT_ and ASSERT_ macros correctly.
  • Ensure tests are thread-safe and don't have race conditions.

docs.title: Performance Validation
docs.text:
• Any change to the core scheduling logic or deque must be validated using the benchmarks in benchmarks/.
  • Document performance impact in PR descriptions.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The Async Task Scheduler Dashboard is a real-time web-based visualization tool for monitoring and analyzing the performance of the task scheduler. It provides insights into:

  • Task Queue Status: Visual representation of task queues across workers by priority level
  • Worker Activity: Real-time monitoring of worker thread performance and CPU usage
  • Performance Metrics: Throughput, latency, and work-stealing efficiency statistics

docs.title: 📊 Queue Status Panel
docs.text:
Displays task distribution across priority levels: - High Priority: Critical tasks (red) - Normal Priority: Standard tasks (orange) - Low Priority: Background tasks (blue)

Metrics include per-worker queue depth and global statistics, helping identify load imbalances.

docs.title: 👷 Worker Activity Panel
docs.text: Real-time worker monitoring showing: - Status: Active (green) or Idle (gray) - CPU Usage: Per-worker CPU utilization with visual progress bars - Tasks Completed: Total tasks processed by each worker - Tasks Stolen: Number of tasks acquired via work-stealing - Trend Charts: Historical CPU usage and task completion trends

docs.title: 📈 Performance Metrics Panel
docs.text: System-wide performance analysis: - Throughput: Tasks processed per second - Work-Stealing Stats: Steals per second and total steal count - Efficiency Ratios: Work-stealing ratio and direct completion rate - System Uptime: Total runtime and average latency

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Node.js 14+
  • The async-task-scheduler compiled and running

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
cd ui
npm install

docs.title: Development Mode
docs.text:
Start both server and client with live reload:

npm run dev

The dashboard will be available at http://localhost:3000

docs.title: Production Build
docs.text:
npm run build
npm start

docs.title: API Integration
docs.text:
The dashboard communicates with the scheduler via REST API endpoints:

Endpoint Description GET /api/metrics Overall scheduler metrics snapshot GET /api/metrics/queue-status Task queue status by worker and priority GET /api/metrics/workers Worker statistics and CPU usage GET /api/metrics/performance System performance and throughput metrics GET /api/metrics/history Historical data for trend analysis GET /api/health Server health check

docs.title: Step 1: Extend Scheduler with Metrics Exposure
docs.text:
Add methods to your scheduler to expose metrics:

// In scheduler.hpp or worker.hpp
struct WorkerMetrics {
    uint64_t tasksCompleted;
    uint64_t tasksStolen;
    double cpuUsage;
    WorkerStatus status;
};

class Scheduler {
    std::vector<WorkerMetrics> getWorkerMetrics() const;
    QueueStatus getQueueStatus() const;
    // ...
};

docs.title: Step 2: Create Metrics Bridge
docs.text:
Implement a bridge to expose metrics via HTTP (shared memory, IPC, or direct C++ binding):

// Example: REST server using a library like crow or pistache
auto metricsRoute = [scheduler]() {
    auto metrics = scheduler->getMetrics();
    return json::serialize(metrics);
};

docs.title: Step 3: Update MetricsCollector
docs.text:
Modify ui/server/metricsCollector.js to connect to your scheduler:

import { MetricsCollector as CppMetrics } from '../scheduler-binding/index.js';

class MetricsCollector {
    constructor() {
        this.cppMetrics = new CppMetrics();
    }

    getMetrics() {
        return this.cppMetrics.getMetrics();
    }
}

docs.title: Identifying Load Imbalance
docs.text:
• Indicator: High variance in queue depths across workers
  • Action: Check work-stealing ratio; high ratios indicate uneven distribution

docs.title: Detecting Bottlenecks
docs.text:
• Indicator: Consistent high CPU on one or two workers with idle workers present
  • Action: Review task affinity and consider load balancing adjustments

docs.title: Optimizing Throughput
docs.text:
• Indicator: Low work-stealing ratio despite idle workers
  • Action: Tasks may be too large; consider decomposition or priority adjustments

docs.title: Latency Analysis
docs.text:
• Indicator: High average latency with low throughput
  • Action: Check for priority starvation; ensure High priority tasks are processed promptly

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│   React Dashboard (Client)              │
│  ├─ QueueStatus Component              │
│  ├─ WorkerActivity Component           │
│  └─ PerformanceMetrics Component       │
└──────────────────┬──────────────────────┘
                   │ HTTP (REST)
┌──────────────────▼──────────────────────┐
│   Express Server (Node.js)              │
│  ├─ /api/metrics                       │
│  ├─ /api/metrics/queue-status          │
│  ├─ /api/metrics/workers               │
│  └─ /api/metrics/performance           │
└──────────────────┬──────────────────────┘
                   │ IPC/Shared Memory
┌──────────────────▼──────────────────────┐
│   Async Task Scheduler (C++)            │
│  ├─ WorkStealingDeque                  │
│  ├─ Worker Threads                     │
│  └─ Metrics Collection                 │
└─────────────────────────────────────────┘

docs.title: Dashboard not connecting
docs.text:
• Verify Express server is running: curl http://localhost:3001/api/health
  • Check REACT_APP_API_BASE environment variable
  • Ensure CORS is properly configured

docs.title: No metrics displayed
docs.text:
• Verify scheduler is running and exposing metrics
  • Check browser console for API errors
  • Review MetricsCollector implementation

docs.title: Performance degradation
docs.text:
• Reduce polling frequency (currently 1 second)
  • Consider WebSocket implementation for lower overhead
  • Profile React component render times

docs.title: Future Enhancements
docs.text:
• [ ] WebSocket Support: Real-time push updates instead of polling
  • [ ] Historical Analysis: Long-term trend analysis and export
  • [ ] Custom Alerts: Configurable thresholds and notifications
  • [ ] Task Profiling: Per-task execution time and memory usage
  • [ ] Distributed Monitoring: Multi-scheduler aggregation
  • [ ] OpenTelemetry Integration: Standards-based metrics export

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Create a .env.local file in ui/server/ to customize:

PORT=3001
NODE_ENV=production
SCHEDULER_HOST=localhost
SCHEDULER_PORT=5000
POLLING_INTERVAL=1000

docs.title: Performance Considerations
docs.text:
• Polling Interval: Default 1 second; increase for slower networks
  • History Size: Default 300 data points (~5 minutes at 1Hz)
  • Chart Updates: Debounced to prevent excessive renders
  • Memory Usage: Minimal; scales linearly with worker count

See README.md for additional technical details.

docs.title: Integration Guide for Dashboard
docs.text: This guide explains how to integrate the real-time dashboard with your async-task-scheduler implementation.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The dashboard communicates with the scheduler through a REST API. The integration involves:

  1. Implementing the Metrics Interface in your scheduler
  2. Creating a Metrics Server to expose metrics via HTTP
  3. Connecting the Dashboard to the metrics server

docs.title: Step 1: Implement IMetricsCollector
docs.text:
The metrics.hpp header defines the interface for exposing scheduler metrics. Implement this interface in your scheduler:

#include "scheduler/metrics.hpp"
#include "scheduler/scheduler.hpp"

class SchedulerMetricsCollector : public scheduler::IMetricsCollector {
private:
    Scheduler& scheduler_;

public:
    explicit SchedulerMetricsCollector(Scheduler& scheduler)
        : scheduler_(scheduler) {}

    WorkerMetrics getWorkerMetrics(uint32_t workerId) const override {
        auto& worker = scheduler_.getWorker(workerId);
        return WorkerMetrics{
            workerId,
            worker.isActive() ? WorkerStatus::Active : WorkerStatus::Idle,
            worker.getTasksCompleted(),
            worker.getTasksStolen(),
            worker.getCpuUsage(),
            worker.getQueueSize(Priority::High),
            worker.getQueueSize(Priority::Normal),
            worker.getQueueSize(Priority::Low),
        };
    }

    std::vector<WorkerMetrics> getAllWorkerMetrics() const override {
        std::vector<WorkerMetrics> metrics;
        for (uint32_t i = 0; i < scheduler_.getWorkerCount(); ++i) {
            metrics.push_back(getWorkerMetrics(i));
        }
        return metrics;
    }

    QueueStatus getQueueStatus() const override {
        // Implementation
    }

    PerformanceMetrics getPerformanceMetrics() const override {
        // Implementation
    }

    // ... other methods
};

docs.title: Step 2: Create a Metrics REST Server
docs.text:
You can use libraries like Pistache or crow to create a REST API.

Example with crow:

#include <crow_all.hpp>
#include "scheduler/metrics.hpp"

int main() {
    Scheduler scheduler(4); // 4 worker threads
    SchedulerMetricsCollector metrics(scheduler);

    crow::SimpleApp app;

    CROW_ROUTE(app, "/api/metrics")
    ([&metrics]() {
        auto perf = metrics.getPerformanceMetrics();
        return crow::response(crow::status::ok, json_serialize(perf));
    });

    CROW_ROUTE(app, "/api/metrics/workers")
    ([&metrics]() {
        auto workers = metrics.getAllWorkerMetrics();
        return crow::response(crow::status::ok, json_serialize(workers));
    });

    CROW_ROUTE(app, "/api/metrics/queue-status")
    ([&metrics]() {
        auto status = metrics.getQueueStatus();
        return crow::response(crow::status::ok, json_serialize(status));
    });

    app.port(3001).multithreaded().run();
    return 0;
}

docs.title: Step 3: Configure the Dashboard
docs.text:
1. Build the Dashboard: 

    cd ui/client
npm install
npm run build

  2. Start the Metrics Server: 

    ./your_metrics_server

  3. Serve the Dashboard: 

    cd ui/server
NODE_ENV=production PORT=3000 npm start

  4. Access the Dashboard: Open http://localhost:3000 in your browser

docs.title: API Endpoints Reference
docs.text: All endpoints return JSON responses.

docs.title: GET /api/metrics
docs.text:
Returns overall scheduler metrics including throughput and steal counts.

Response: 

{
  "uptime": 12345678,
  "totalTasksProcessed": 1000000,
  "totalTasksStolen": 50000,
  "tasksPerSecond": 1000.5,
  "stealsPerSecond": 50.2,
  "averageLatency": 75.3,
  "averageCpuUsage": 65.2
}

docs.title: GET /api/metrics/workers
docs.text:
Returns per-worker metrics for all workers.

Response: 

[
  {
    "id": 0,
    "status": "active",
    "tasksCompleted": 250000,
    "tasksStolen": 12500,
    "cpuUsage": 85.3,
    "highPriorityQueued": 5,
    "normalPriorityQueued": 15,
    "lowPriorityQueued": 2
  },
  ...
]

docs.title: GET /api/metrics/queue-status
docs.text:
Returns global queue status and per-worker breakdown.

Response: 

{
  "totalQueued": 22,
  "highPriorityTotal": 5,
  "normalPriorityTotal": 15,
  "lowPriorityTotal": 2,
  "workers": [...]
}

docs.title: GET /api/metrics/performance
docs.text:
Returns performance metrics and efficiency calculations.

Response: 

{
  "throughput": {
    "tasksPerSecond": 1000.5,
    "stealsPerSecond": 50.2
  },
  "totalTasksProcessed": 1000000,
  "totalTasksStolen": 50000,
  "uptime": 12345,
  "averageLatency": 75.3
}

docs.title: Metrics JSON Schema
docs.text: For custom serialization, use these schemas:

docs.title: WorkerStatus Enum
docs.text:
• "active" - Worker is processing tasks
  • "idle" - Worker is idle
  • "suspended" - Worker is suspended

docs.title: WorkerMetrics
docs.text:
struct WorkerMetrics {
    uint32_t id;
    std::string status;           // "active", "idle", or "suspended"
    uint64_t tasksCompleted;
    uint64_t tasksStolen;
    double cpuUsage;              // 0.0 - 100.0
    uint32_t highPriorityQueued;
    uint32_t normalPriorityQueued;
    uint32_t lowPriorityQueued;
};

docs.title: Dashboard shows "Connection Error"
docs.text:
• Verify the metrics server is running on port 3001
  • Check CORS headers: ensure Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * is set
  • Test with curl: curl -s http://localhost:3001/api/metrics | jq

docs.title: Metrics are outdated
docs.text:
• Ensure metrics collection is enabled: metricsCollector.setMetricsEnabled(true)
  • Reduce polling interval in dashboard if needed
  • Check network latency

docs.title: High CPU usage in dashboard
docs.text:
• Reduce polling frequency from 1s to 2-5s
  • Consider implementing WebSocket for push updates
  • Profile React components with Chrome DevTools

docs.title: Performance Considerations
docs.text:
• Polling Interval: Default 1 second; safe for most systems
  • Metrics Collection Overhead: Minimal (<1% overhead per metrics query)
  • Memory: Metrics snapshots are lightweight (~1KB per worker)
  • Network: Each metrics query is <2KB

docs.title: Future: WebSocket Integration
docs.text:
For lower latency and reduced server load:

// Server-side (crow)
CROW_WEBSOCKET_ROUTE(app, "/ws/metrics")
.onopen([](crow::websocket::connection& conn) {
    // Start streaming metrics
})
.onclose([](crow::websocket::connection& conn) {
    // Stop streaming
})
.onmessage([](crow::websocket::connection& conn, const std::string& data) {
    // Handle client requests
});

// Client-side (JavaScript)
const ws = new WebSocket('ws://localhost:3001/ws/metrics');
ws.onmessage = (event) => {
    const metrics = JSON.parse(event.data);
    updateDashboard(metrics);
};

docs.title: Example: Complete Integration
docs.text: See docs/examples/metrics-server.cpp for a complete example of implementing and serving metrics.

docs.title: Support
docs.text: For issues or questions: 1. Check the Dashboard Documentation 2. Review Architecture for system design 3. Examine Copilot Instructions for AI-assisted debugging

docs.title: Packaging & Installer Generation
docs.text: This document explains how to create installers/packages for the Async Task Scheduler and how the CI workflow produces artifacts.

docs.title: CI Packaging (GitHub Actions)
docs.text:
A workflow is provided at .github/workflows/packaging.yml. It runs on tagged pushes (v* tags) and on manual dispatch. For each platform (Windows, macOS, Linux) it:

  • builds the C++ project with CMake
  • builds the UI (ui/client)
  • runs platform packaging scripts to produce artifacts
  • uploads artifacts to the workflow run

Windows builds use NSIS (if available) to create an .exe installer. The NSIS script template is at packaging/windows/installer.nsi and a helper script packaging/windows/package.ps1 produces the staging directory and invokes makensis.

On macOS and Linux the CI creates a compressed tarball of the build outputs and ui/client/build in dist/.

docs.title: Windows (NSIS)
docs.text:
Requirements: - NSIS (makensis) on PATH

From repository root:

# Run packaging script (PowerShell)
.\packaging\windows\package.ps1 -BuildDir build -UiDir ui -OutDir dist

If makensis is not installed, the script will generate installer_generated.nsi in the dist folder; install NSIS and run makensis manually.

docs.title: macOS / Linux
docs.text:
chmod +x packaging/package.sh
./packaging/package.sh build ui dist

This produces a dist/*.tar.gz file containing the scheduler binaries and the static UI build.

docs.title: Customization
docs.text:
• Edit packaging/windows/installer.nsi to change install paths, shortcuts, or add registry entries.
  • Update packaging/windows/package.ps1 to include additional files in the staging directory.
  • Add code signing steps to the CI workflow when you have a signing certificate (recommended for Windows installers).

docs.title: CI Integration Notes
docs.text:
• The workflow uses the actions/upload-artifact step to persist build artifacts.
  • To produce a signed installer on CI, add signing actions/steps in the packaging job for the appropriate platform.

docs.title: Next Steps (Optional)
docs.text:
• Add an MSI build (WiX) for enterprise installs.
  • Add code-signing to the CI pipeline.
  • Add platform-specific packages (deb, rpm, dmg, msi) as needed.

docs.title: Setup System Documentation
docs.text: This document explains the unified setup and launch system for the Async Task Scheduler project.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The project provides multiple entry points for setup and launch, all coordinated to ensure a smooth developer experience:

  • PowerShell Script (setup.ps1) - Primary for Windows developers
  • Bash Script (setup.sh) - Primary for macOS/Linux developers
  • Node.js Script (setup.js) - Cross-platform alternative
  • Batch Launcher (launch.bat) - Windows quick access

docs.title: Single Entry Point Philosophy
docs.text:
All scripts implement the same workflow regardless of platform:

User Command
    ↓
Platform-Specific Script
    ↓
1. Check Prerequisites
    ↓
2. Install C++ Dependencies
    ↓
3. Install Node.js Dependencies
    ↓
4. Build C++ Project
    ↓
5. Configure Dashboard
    ↓
Ready to Run

docs.title: Implementation Details
docs.text: Each script performs these core operations:

docs.title: 1. Prerequisite Checking
docs.text:
• CMake 3.20+
  • C++ Compiler (MSVC, GCC, or Clang)
  • Node.js 14+
  • vcpkg (optional, auto-cloned if needed)

docs.title: 2. C++ Dependency Installation
docs.text: vcpkg bootstrap → vcpkg install (via vcpkg.json)

docs.title: 3. Node.js Dependency Installation
docs.text:
npm install (root)
    ↓
npm install -w server
    ↓
npm install -w client

docs.title: 4. C++ Build
docs.text:
cmake configure (with vcpkg toolchain)
    ↓
cmake build --parallel

docs.title: 5. Dashboard Configuration
docs.text:
• Ensures UI dependencies are installed
  • Creates .env from template if needed
  • Validates server configuration

docs.title: Script Comparison
docs.text: Feature PowerShell Bash Node.js Batch Platform Windows macOS/Linux All Windows Features Full Full Full Wrapper Color Output ✓ ✓ ✓ Via PS1 Error Handling ✓ ✓ ✓ Via PS1 Parallel Build ✓ ✓ ✓ Via PS1 Help Text ✓ ✓ ✓ Via PS1

docs.title: PowerShell (setup.ps1)
docs.text:
Advantages: - Native Windows shell - Integrated error handling - Full featured menu system - Environment management

Modes: 

.\setup.ps1                    # Full setup
.\setup.ps1 -Mode setup        # Explicit
.\setup.ps1 -Mode dev          # Dev + run
.\setup.ps1 -Mode build        # Build only
.\setup.ps1 -Mode run          # Run only
.\setup.ps1 -Mode clean        # Clean artifacts
.\setup.ps1 -Help              # Show help

docs.title: Bash (setup.sh)
docs.text:
Advantages: - Native Unix shell (macOS/Linux) - POSIX compliance - Portable across systems

Usage: 

./setup.sh                     # Full setup
./setup.sh dev                 # Dev + run
./setup.sh build               # Build only
./setup.sh run                 # Run only
./setup.sh clean               # Clean
./setup.sh help                # Show help

docs.title: Node.js (setup.js)
docs.text:
Advantages: - True cross-platform (Windows/macOS/Linux) - No shell dependency - Direct package management

Usage: 

node setup.js                  # Full setup
node setup.js --dev            # Dev + run
node setup.js --build          # Build only
node setup.js --run            # Run only
node setup.js --clean          # Clean
node setup.js --help           # Show help

docs.title: Batch (launch.bat)
docs.text:
Advantages: - Native Windows compatibility - Simple interface - Delegates to PowerShell for actual work

Usage: 

launch.bat setup
launch.bat dev
launch.bat build
launch.bat run
launch.bat clean
launch.bat help

docs.title: Full Setup (Default)
docs.text:
.\setup.ps1              # All setup steps

What happens: 1. Checks all prerequisites 2. If missing: displays installation links 3. Installs vcpkg if needed 4. Runs vcpkg install 5. Installs root npm dependencies 6. Installs server npm dependencies 7. Installs client npm dependencies 8. Configures CMake with vcpkg toolchain 9. Builds C++ project in Release mode 10. Displays completion message with next steps

Time: 5-15 minutes (depending on system and internet)

docs.title: Development Mode
docs.text:
.\setup.ps1 -Mode dev    # Setup + run

What happens: 1. Full setup (as above) 2. Starts Express server (port 3001) 3. Starts React dev server (port 3000) 4. Opens dashboard in browser (if available) 5. Keeps running with hot-reload

Access: - Dashboard: http://localhost:3000 - API: http://localhost:3001

docs.title: Build Only
docs.text:
.\setup.ps1 -Mode build

What happens: 1. Skips prerequisite checks 2. Skips dependency installation 3. Runs CMake configuration 4. Builds C++ project 5. Reports success/failure

Use when: Dependencies already installed, just need rebuild

docs.title: Run Only
docs.text:
.\setup.ps1 -Mode run

What happens: 1. Checks if builds exist 2. If not: builds them 3. Checks if node_modules exists 4. If not: installs dependencies 5. Starts dashboard 6. Starts API server

Use when: Everything already built, just need to run

docs.title: Clean
docs.text:
.\setup.ps1 -Mode clean

What happens: 1. Removes build/ directory 2. Removes all node_modules directories 3. Removes generated artifacts

Use when: Starting fresh or troubleshooting build issues

docs.title: Server Configuration
docs.text:
The Express server reads configuration from ui/server/.env:

# Copy template to create actual config
cp ui/server/.env.example ui/server/.env

# Edit as needed
PORT=3001
NODE_ENV=development
REACT_APP_API_BASE=http://localhost:3001

docs.title: CMake Configuration
docs.text: CMake settings are managed through: - CMakeLists.txt - main build configuration - vcpkg toolchain - dependency management - -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release - build mode

docs.title: Script Fails to Execute
docs.text:
PowerShell (Windows): 

# If execution policy prevents running:
Set-ExecutionPolicy -ExecutionPolicy RemoteSigned -Scope CurrentUser

Bash (macOS/Linux): 

# Make script executable:
chmod +x setup.sh

docs.title: Prerequisites Missing
docs.text: Scripts will display installation URLs and continue. Install missing tools and re-run.

docs.title: Build Fails
docs.text:
# Clean and rebuild:
.\setup.ps1 -Mode clean
.\setup.ps1 -Mode build

docs.title: Port Already in Use
docs.text:
Edit ui/server/.env: 

PORT=3002        # Change from 3001

Then restart: 

.\setup.ps1 -Mode run

docs.title: Integration with CI/CD
docs.text:
For automated builds (GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, etc.):

# Example: GitHub Actions
- name: Setup Project
  run: node setup.js --build

- name: Run Tests
  run: ./build/Release/scheduler_test.exe

docs.title: Setup Scripts
docs.text:
• setup.ps1 - PowerShell (Windows primary)
  • setup.sh - Bash (macOS/Linux primary)
  • setup.js - Node.js (cross-platform)
  • launch.bat - Batch wrapper (Windows convenience)

docs.title: Configuration Files
docs.text:
• .gitattributes - Cross-platform line ending management
  • .env.example - Environment template
  • vcpkg.json - C++ dependencies
  • package.json (root & workspaces) - Node.js dependencies

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text:
• docs/QUICKSTART.md - Quick start guide
  • docs/setup-system.md - This file
  • README.md - Main project documentation

docs.title: Design Principles
docs.text:
The setup system follows these principles:

  1. Single Responsibility - Each script does one thing well
  2. Cross-Platform - Works on Windows, macOS, and Linux
  3. Deterministic - Same input always produces same output
  4. Informative - Clear output and error messages
  5. Failfast - Stops on first error, doesn't try to recover
  6. Idempotent - Safe to run multiple times
  7. Well-Documented - Help text and documentation included

docs.title: Adding New Setup Steps
docs.text:
To add a new setup step:

  1. Identify the step - What does it do?
  2. Implement in all scripts - Add to all 4 scripts
  3. Integrate into workflow - Add to appropriate mode(s)
  4. Test on all platforms - Windows, macOS, Linux
  5. Update documentation - docs/QUICKSTART.md, etc.

Example: Adding a step to validate the build

# PowerShell
function Validate-Build {
    Write-Header "Validating Build"
    # validation logic
}

# Call in Run-Setup
Run-Setup() {
    # ... existing steps ...
    Validate-Build
}

docs.title: Parallel Compilation
docs.text:
• Uses cmake --build ... --parallel with automatic core detection
  • Significantly speeds up C++ builds on multi-core systems

docs.title: Npm Silent Mode
docs.text:
• Uses npm install --silent to reduce console spam
  • Useful for CI/CD pipelines

docs.title: Caching
docs.text:
• Scripts check if builds already exist
  • Only rebuilds when necessary
  • Can use --run mode to skip rebuild

docs.title: Future Enhancements
docs.text:
Potential improvements to the setup system:

  • [ ] Automatic IDE project generation (Visual Studio, Xcode, VSCode)
  • [ ] Docker containerization for guaranteed environment
  • [ ] GitHub Actions matrix testing on multiple OS/compiler versions
  • [ ] Interactive configuration wizard for advanced options
  • [ ] Metrics collection for setup performance analysis
  • [ ] Update checking to warn about new versions
  • [ ] WebSocket support for real-time build output

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
The unified setup system provides:

✅ Multiple entry points - Choose your preferred shell/language ✅ Consistent behavior - Same workflow on all platforms ✅ Easy onboarding - One command to get started ✅ Flexibility - Different modes for different needs ✅ Good UX - Clear output and error messages ✅ Well-documented - Help text and guides included

Whether you're on Windows with PowerShell, macOS with Bash, or prefer Node.js, the setup experience is consistent and straightforward.

Quick Reference: 

Windows:   .\setup.ps1 -Mode dev
macOS/Linux: ./setup.sh dev
Any OS:    node setup.js --dev

Then open http://localhost:3000 🚀

docs.title: Async Task Scheduler - User Guide
docs.text: This guide provides a comprehensive overview of how to integrate and use the Async Task Scheduler in your C++20 projects.

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
1. Core Concepts
  2. Getting Started
  3. Task Management
  4. Priority Levels
  5. Work Stealing
  6. Best Practices

docs.title: Core Concepts
docs.text:
The async-task-scheduler is a high-performance, multi-threaded executor designed for low-latency systems.

  • Scheduler: The central entry point. It manages a pool of worker threads and distributes tasks.
  • Worker: An internal thread that executes tasks. Each worker has its own local queues to minimize lock contention.
  • Task: A C++20 coroutine that represents a unit of work.
  • Work-Stealing: A strategy where idle workers "steal" tasks from the queues of busy workers, ensuring optimal CPU utilization.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text:
To use the scheduler, include the main header:

#include <scheduler/scheduler.hpp>
#include <scheduler/task.hpp>
#include <iostream>

using namespace scheduler;

// Define a simple task using C++20 coroutines
Task my_task(int id) {
    std::cout << "Task " << id << " is running on thread " << std::this_thread::get_id() << std::endl;
    co_return;
}

int main() {
    // Create a scheduler with 4 worker threads
    Scheduler scheduler(4);

    // Schedule tasks
    for (int i = 0; i < 10; ++i) {
        scheduler.schedule(std::make_unique<Task>(my_task(i)), Priority::Normal);
    }

    // The scheduler will automatically clean up when it goes out of scope
    return 0;
}

docs.title: Creating Tasks
docs.text:
Tasks must be defined as coroutines returning scheduler::Task. 

Task compute_heavy_work() {
    // Do something expensive
    int result = 0;
    for(int i = 0; i < 1000000; ++i) result += i;

    std::cout << "Result: " << result << std::endl;
    co_return;
}

docs.title: Scheduling Tasks
docs.text:
Tasks are scheduled using the schedule method. You must pass a std::unique_ptr<Task>.

scheduler.schedule(std::make_unique<Task>(compute_heavy_work()));

docs.title: Priority Levels
docs.text:
The scheduler supports three priority levels: - Priority::High: Tasks that should be executed as soon as possible. - Priority::Normal: Standard tasks (default). - Priority::Low: Background tasks that can wait for idle time.

scheduler.schedule(std::make_unique<Task>(urgent_task()), Priority::High);
scheduler.schedule(std::make_unique<Task>(background_task()), Priority::Low);

Workers prioritize their own High queue, then steal High tasks from others, before moving to Normal and then Low priorities.

docs.title: Work Stealing
docs.text: Work-stealing is enabled by default. It allows the system to balance load dynamically. If a thread finishes all its tasks, it will look at the back of other workers' deques to find more work. This is implemented using a lock-free WorkStealingDeque to minimize overhead.

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Minimize Task Granularity: Tasks should be small enough to be balanced effectively, but not so small that the scheduling overhead dominates.
  2. Avoid Blocking: Do not call blocking operations (like std::this_thread::sleep_for or heavy I/O) inside a task if possible, as it ties up a worker thread. Use co_await with asynchronous I/O if available.
  3. Use the Right Priority: Avoid flooding the High priority queue, as it may starve Normal and Low priority tasks.
  4. Pre-allocate Tasks: If performance is critical, consider using a task pool to avoid frequent allocations of std::unique_ptr<Task>.
